---
title: "Recipes: choosing and composing movers"
---

The narrative pages built the machinery — representations, pull-back resampling,
similarity metrics, optimisers, the diffeomorphic stack. This page is the
**catalogue**: the public functions in `nitrix.register` that assemble that
machinery into end-to-end alignments, the knobs that steer each, and a decision
table from *problem* to *recipe*. Read it as reference — keep it open beside your
code.

The transform ladder of the earlier pages — rigid, affine, diffeomorphic,
surface — is a ladder of *representations*. The recipes are the ladder of
*entry points*: the functions you actually call. Each one fixes a representation,
a similarity, and an optimiser, and exposes the handful of decisions left to you
as keyword arguments and spec fields. The single idea that organises every one of
them is that a recipe is a **thin, pure function** `(moving, fixed) -> NamedTuple`
over a **frozen configuration record** (a `*Spec`): the spec is static config that
rides `jit`, the output is plain arrays. That contract is worth stating once in
full:

- **Inputs** are single-channel arrays `(*spatial,)` (2-D or 3-D), *not* PyTree
  modules. `moving` is the image you move; `fixed` is the target you align it to.
  The recovered map pulls `moving` **back** onto the `fixed` grid (the convention
  from [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-pullback)).
- **Outputs** are `NamedTuple`s of arrays — `RegistrationResult`, `VolregResult`,
  `BBRResult`, `PipelineResult`, `DiffeomorphicResult`, `SyNResult`. No I/O, no
  atlas objects; image-file handling is out of scope (it lives downstream).
- **Configuration** is a frozen, hashable `@dataclass` (`RegistrationSpec`,
  `SyNSpec`, `DemonsSpec`, `BBRSpec`). Frozen so it can ride a `jit` static
  argument; you build one, hand it in, and never mutate it.

Our running examples are the four canonical neuroimaging questions: **align a
subject's T1 to MNI** (rigid → affine → SyN), **realign a moving fMRI series**
(`volreg`), **register an EPI to its T1 across modalities** (`bbr_register` or a
cross-modal pipeline), and **deform one anatomy onto another** (Demons / SyN).

@fig-recipes-decision routes a problem to a recipe; the
[decision table](#sec-specs) at the end is the same map in tabular form.

```{python}
#| label: fig-recipes-decision
#| fig-cap: "From problem to recipe. Same-modality SSD takes the inverse-compositional fast path; cross-modal work uses MI / CR / LNCC. The bottom band shows the three special cases: a 4-D series (volreg), a tissue boundary (bbr_register), and anisotropic or differing grids (WorldSpace)."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import FancyBboxPatch, FancyArrowPatch

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.4, 6.6))
ax.set_xlim(-2, 102); ax.set_ylim(0, 100); ax.axis("off")

def box(x, y, w, h, text, fc, fontsize=9, ec="#33373d"):
    ax.add_patch(FancyBboxPatch(
        (x - w / 2, y - h / 2), w, h,
        boxstyle="round,pad=1.4,rounding_size=2.2",
        linewidth=1.2, edgecolor=ec, facecolor=fc, zorder=2))
    ax.text(x, y, text, ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=fontsize, zorder=3, color="#15181c")

def arrow(x0, y0, x1, y1, label=None, lx=0, ly=0):
    ax.add_patch(FancyArrowPatch(
        (x0, y0), (x1, y1), arrowstyle="-|>", mutation_scale=13,
        linewidth=1.2, color="#55606b", zorder=1, shrinkA=2, shrinkB=2))
    if label:
        ax.text((x0 + x1) / 2 + lx, (y0 + y1) / 2 + ly, label,
                ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.6, style="italic",
                color="#444b53", zorder=4,
                bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.15", fc="white", ec="none"))

start = "#dfe7f0"; decis = "#fbf0d6"; leaf = "#dce9dd"; leaf2 = "#e8ddec"
box(50, 94, 40, 9, "problem: align A -> B", start, 10)
box(50, 79, 34, 9, "same modality?", decis)
arrow(50, 89.5, 50, 83.5)
box(24, 62, 30, 9, "linear only?", decis)
box(76, 62, 30, 9, "linear only?", decis)
arrow(33, 79, 26, 66.5, "yes (SSD)", -1, 4)
arrow(67, 79, 74, 66.5, "no (MI/CR/LNCC)", 0, 4)
box(13, 44, 22, 11, "rigid_register\naffine_register\nSSD (IC fast)", leaf, 8)
box(40, 44, 24, 13, "syn_pipeline\nor demons (SSD)\n/ greedy_syn", leaf, 8)
arrow(20, 57.5, 14, 49.5, "yes", -3, 0)
arrow(28, 57.5, 38, 50.5, "no", 3, 0)
box(63, 44, 22, 13, "rigid/affine\nMI or CR\nor bbr_register", leaf2, 8)
box(88, 44, 21, 13, "syn_pipeline\nMI linear +\nLNCC SyN", leaf2, 8)
arrow(70, 57.5, 64, 50.5, "yes", -3, 0)
arrow(82, 57.5, 87, 50.5, "no", 3, 0)
box(19, 23, 30, 10, "4-D series\n(motion)?  -> volreg", "#f3dede", 8.4)
box(54, 23, 30, 10, "surface boundary\n(EPI<->T1)? -> bbr_register", "#f3dede", 8.0)
box(86, 23, 20, 10, "anisotropic /\ndiff. grids?\n-> WorldSpace", "#e3e3e8", 7.6)
ax.text(50, 7,
        "leaves name the public recipe; SSD -> the inverse-compositional fast "
        "path,\nMI/CR/LNCC -> the cross-modal / bias-robust path",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.8, color="#555", style="italic")
ax.set_title("Routing a registration problem to a recipe", fontsize=12, pad=6)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

A note on **execution class** (the tags from the contract): the recipes
themselves are **DIFF-JAX** — jittable and (in the default `mode='fixed'`)
reverse-differentiable. A few constructors do host-side, data-dependent work
*before* the device kernel — branching on a Python-level shape or dtype:
`affine_register` warns and *shortens its own pyramid* on a tiny grid
(`_cap_levels`, which inspects `fixed.shape`) and `apply_transform` branches on
the array `dtype` to pick its kernel. Those are **HOST-CTOR** steps — fine at
call time, but never call them inside a jitted loop. (`init='moment'` is
data-dependent — it reads the images' intensity moments — but those moments are
computed *on device* from pure `jnp` ops, so the moment init traces and jits;
it is a one-shot construction, not a per-iteration cost.) The all-positive
`jacobian_det` check (`det.min() > 0`) is **HOST-QA** — it returns a Python bool,
so it belongs *after* a fit, never inside a traced loop. The rule throughout:
*spec choices are static; image data is traced.*

## Matrix recipes: rigid, affine, apply, and the staged pipeline {#sec-matrix-recipes}

These are the linear rungs of the ladder — the recipes that recover a single
homogeneous matrix $\mathbf A$. Their shared engine is the coarse-to-fine driver
from [optimization](optimization.qmd#sec-coarse-fine): build a Gaussian pyramid
of `fixed`, minimise the similarity over the transform's Lie parameters $\theta$
at the coarsest level, rescale and warm-start the next finer level, repeat.

### `rigid_register` — six degrees of freedom

`rigid_register(moving, fixed, *, spec, space, method, init, init_transform,
mask, winsorize, histogram_match)` recovers a rigid map: a rotation and a
translation, the transform of a body that does not deform. In 3-D that is
6 parameters (an axis-angle vector $\omega$ and a translation $t$), in 2-D it is
3. The parametrisation is the Lie chart of
[transforms](transforms.qmd#sec-lie-exp): $\theta = (\omega, t)$, and the matrix
is $\mathbf A = \exp\,\theta$ via the closed-form Rodrigues formula (`Rigid` in
`_model.py`). Optimising over $\theta$ rather than over the nine entries of a
rotation matrix is what keeps every iterate a valid rotation for free.

What does it return? A `RegistrationResult` with four fields:

- `matrix` — the **self-contained** homogeneous transform, fixed → moving. "Self-
  contained" means the grid centre the warp rotates about is *baked in*, so
  `apply_affine(coords, matrix)` reproduces the warp directly and the matrix
  composes with another result. In `IndexSpace` it is the full-resolution
  fixed-voxel → moving-voxel index map; in `WorldSpace` the world → world map (mm).
- `params` — the raw **about-origin** Lie parameters $\theta$ at full resolution.
- `warped` — `moving` resampled onto the `fixed` grid by `matrix`.
- `cost_history` — the concatenated per-level optimiser cost traces.

The reason `matrix` and `params` differ is the centring problem from
[transforms](transforms.qmd#sec-centering): a rotation applied about the origin
moves an off-origin brain by a translation you did not intend, so the recipe
internally rotates about the grid centre $c$ and *conjugates* the raw
`model.exp(params)` by that centre to produce the self-contained `matrix`. Keep
`params` if you want the bare chart coordinates; use `matrix` for everything else.

**`init` — where the optimiser starts.** A second-order optimiser converges fast
*inside* the basin of the right minimum but cannot climb out of the wrong one.
The simplest start, `init='identity'` (the default), assumes the images already
roughly overlap. When they do not — a brain off-centre by a third of the field
of view — that single zero start can sit outside the narrow capture range and the
fit fails silently. `init='moment'` is the cheap robust fix: it matches the
**intensity-weighted centroids** (a translation) of the two images, treating each
image as a density. Writing $w = \max(I, 0)$ for the non-negative weights and the
voxel grid $x$,

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_x w(x)\,x}{\sum_x w(x)}, \qquad
\sigma_d^2 = \frac{\sum_x w(x)\,(x_d - \bar{x}_d)^2}{\sum_x w(x)}
$$ {#eq-recipes-moments}

are the centroid $\bar{x}$ and per-axis spread $\sigma_d$ — the first two moments
of the image-as-density (with non-negative weights $w=\max(I,0)$ and a small
$+10^{-12}$ guard on the normalising mass, from `_image_moments`). The moment
init sets the translation so $\bar{x}_f$ maps onto $\bar{x}_m$. For rigid it
deliberately does **not** add a rotation: matching moments cannot fix the
principal-axis *sign*, so a rotation guess risks a *worse* start. (For affine it
additionally matches the spread as a diagonal scale; see below.) `init='moment'`
is `IndexSpace`-only — the moment matrix is built in voxel coordinates — and is a
one-shot, data-dependent (but device-traced) construction, so build it once at
call time rather than inside a jitted loop.

**`init_transform` — warm-starting from a prior result.** Pass a previous
recipe's `result.matrix` and it takes precedence over `init`: the recipe
de-centres the self-contained matrix back to about-origin coordinates and rescales
the translation to the coarsest pyramid level (the driver upscales it back as it
climbs). This is the hook the staged pipeline uses — affine warm-started from
rigid. `IndexSpace`-only.

**`space` — index vs world.** `IndexSpace()` (the default) reads $\theta$
directly in voxel coordinates and assumes `moving` and `fixed` share a roughly
isotropic voxel grid; it is the leaner, fully-on-device path (no general matrix
inverse, so no cuSolver fallback). `WorldSpace(fixed_affine=..., moving_affine=...)`
carries each image's voxel → world affine and optimises the world → world map, so
a transform that is rigid in *physical* space is recovered correctly under
anisotropic voxels or differing grids. The rule of thumb: a rigid transform in
index space *shears* in physical space when voxels are anisotropic — reach for
`WorldSpace` then. See [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-spaces).

**`method` — the inverse-compositional fast path.** This is the single most
important performance lever in the linear recipes, so it earns a paragraph.
The forward Gauss-Newton step re-linearises the *moving* image every iteration:
each iteration costs roughly $P$ tangent warps (one per parameter,
$P = 6$ rigid, $12$ affine) to build the Jacobian, plus the residual warp. The
inverse-compositional scheme (Baker-Matthews; see
[optimization](optimization.qmd#sec-inverse-comp)) instead linearises the
*reference* once per level — its steepest-descent images and the Gauss-Newton
Hessian $H = SD^{\top}SD$ are built a single time — so each iteration is one warp,
one projection, and a closed-form compositional matrix update. No per-iteration
re-linearisation. Measured on an L4 GPU at identical recovery, that is roughly
**7× at $64^3$, 4× at $128^3$** (the speedup shrinks as the image grows because
the single warp's share of the cost grows). `method`:

- `"auto"` (default) takes the fast path **when its preconditions hold** —
  `IndexSpace` + a least-squares (SSD) metric + the LM/GN/auto optimiser + a
  `Rigid`/`Affine` model + *no mask* — and falls back to the forward path
  otherwise. The forward path is the parity oracle the fast path is tested against.
- `"inverse_compositional"` forces it (and validates the preconditions, raising
  if they do not hold).
- `"forward"` always takes the forward Gauss-Newton / LM path.

A cost `mask` always routes to the forward path: the constant-template Hessian is
a *full-grid* linearisation built once from the whole reference, so it cannot
honour a per-voxel mask. Asking for `method='inverse_compositional'` *with* a mask
is a loud error.

**`mask` — restricting the cost.** A non-negative per-voxel weight on the `fixed`
grid (a brain mask, a lesion exclusion, a field-of-view) restricts or weights the
cost. Out-of-mask voxels are ignored. On the least-squares path the mask enters as
a $\sqrt{\text{mask}}$ row-scaling of the residual Jacobian, so a zero-weight voxel
contributes a zero row and drops out of the Gauss-Newton normal equations; for
MI / CorrelationRatio it gates the joint-histogram scatter, not merely the
reduction.

**`winsorize` / `histogram_match` — intensity conditioning.** Both default *off*
(byte-unchanged). `winsorize=(0.005, 0.995)` clips each image to those percentiles
— outlier-robustness, the fMRIPrep front end. `histogram_match=True` remaps
`moving` onto `fixed`'s intensity distribution (within-modality only). Both
condition the *estimate*, so the returned `warped` is the *conditioned* moving —
to obtain the original intensities, apply `matrix` to the original image with
`apply_transform`. (The package docstring says these are "not yet implemented";
the code disagrees — they are wired into `preprocess_images` and run. Trust the
code.)

### `affine_register` — twelve degrees of freedom

`affine_register` has the same signature plus a `restarts` argument, and adds
six degrees: a general linear block (scale, shear, anisotropic stretch) on top of
the rigid six. In 3-D that is 12 parameters, in 2-D, 6. The linear block is the
*matrix exponential* of a generator (`affine_exp` / `matrix_exp`), which
guarantees the result is invertible and orientation-preserving — you cannot
accidentally optimise into a singular or flipped map. See
[transforms](transforms.qmd#sec-decomposition).

Two affine-specific behaviours:

**The moment init matters more here.** For affine, `init='moment'` matches both
the centroids (translation) and the per-axis spread as a **diagonal scale**
$M = \operatorname{diag}(\sigma_m / \sigma_f)$ from @eq-recipes-moments — no
rotation, for the sign reason above. The affine basin is *narrower* than the rigid
one, and a single zero start fails silently on a large misalignment, so the moment
start (or a rigid warm start) is worth reaching for.

**`restarts` — a basin lever.** `affine_register(..., restarts=k)` runs the
**forward** solve from `k` deterministically-diversified inits (small jitter on
the linear block and translation, fixed PRNG seed) and keeps the lowest-cost
result. It is a pure basin-of-attraction tool: a diverged run scores far worse, so
it is never kept. Historically it doubled as a fix for a non-deterministic GPU
joint-histogram scatter that the 12-DOF BFGS occasionally amplified into a
divergence; that scatter now takes a deterministic one-hot-matmul path at affine
pyramid sizes ($\le 200\text{k}$ voxels/level), so MI / CorrelationRatio recover
reproducibly at `restarts=1`. SSD takes the inverse-compositional path, which is
deterministic and ignores `restarts`. The default is `1`.

::: {.callout-note title="A small-grid safety net (a loud HOST-CTOR fallback)"}
A 12-DOF affine Hessian needs enough voxels per axis to estimate reliably. On a
small image — say $24^3$ — three pyramid levels drop the coarsest grid to
$\le 14^3$, where the affine fit *diverges* to an anti-correlated minimum.
`affine_register` therefore **shortens its own pyramid** (a `_cap_levels` step,
the `_MIN_COARSE_AXIS = 16` floor) and emits an `AffinePyramidDepthWarning` saying
so — a loud, actionable fallback, never a silent one. Rigid (6-DOF) is robust at
small coarse grids and is left untouched. Pass a smaller `spec.levels` to silence
the warning.
:::

### `apply_transform` — moving a second image through a recovered map

`apply_transform(image, result, *, reference_shape, method, mode, cval)` resamples
an array that lives in the **moving** frame of a registration — a second contrast,
a label map, an atlas — onto the reference (fixed) grid by the recovered map. It
**dispatches on the result**: a matrix result (rigid / affine) is applied as a
homogeneous map about the origin (the centre is already baked into `matrix`); a
diffeomorphic result (with a `.displacement` field) builds the sampling grid as
`identity_grid + displacement`. So a `PipelineResult` carrying a SyN displacement
applies the *whole* composite in one call.

The kernel choice is automatic and label-aware (the multi-label resample idea from
[resampling](resampling.qmd#sec-boundary)): for an **integer** `image` it defaults
to `NearestNeighbour` (label-preserving, no interpolation bleed across class
boundaries, with the float round-trip kept label-exact); otherwise `Linear`.
Pass `method=MultiLabel(labels=...)` for anti-aliased multi-label resampling. For
a matrix result, `reference_shape=None` reuses `image.shape` (correct when moving
and fixed share a grid, the `IndexSpace` norm) — pass the fixed shape under
`WorldSpace` or differing grids. This is in-memory resampling only; image-file I/O
is out of scope.

### `syn_pipeline` — rigid → affine → SyN in one call

`syn_pipeline(moving, fixed, *, transform, rigid_spec, affine_spec, syn_spec,
force, affine_restarts)` is the `antsRegistrationSyN` workhorse, composed from the
leaf recipes. It climbs the ladder, and the crucial mechanic is that **each stage
warm-starts the next**:

1. **rigid** (with a moment init) seeds **affine** through `init_transform`;
2. the **affine** result seeds **SyN** through its `init_affine`;
3. so the SyN stage's returned `displacement` is the **full** fixed → moving map —
   linear *and* non-linear, composed.

@fig-recipes-pipeline visualises exactly this growth on a synthetic phantom: at
each stage the warped moving image (grey) closes onto the fixed edges (orange) and
the sampling grid grows from a rigid rotation, to an affine shear, to a curved
deformation. The mean SSD per voxel falls monotonically across the four panels
($0.056 \to 0.036 \to 0.019 \to 0$) — the staged-recovery story in one number.

```{python}
#| label: fig-recipes-pipeline
#| fig-cap: "syn_pipeline on a synthetic phantom. The moving image (grey) is resampled by the cumulative estimate after each stage; the orange contours are the fixed-image edges (the target). The recovered map grows rigid (rotation) -> affine (shear) -> deformable (curved grid), each stage warm-starting the next and explaining away more of the mismatch."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

N = 160
ax_ = np.linspace(-1, 1, N)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(ax_, ax_, indexing="xy")

def make_phantom(Xc, Yc):
    p = np.zeros_like(Xc)
    p[(np.abs(Xc) < 0.62) & (np.abs(Yc) < 0.78)] = 0.4
    p[(Xc**2 + Yc**2) < 0.30**2] = 0.9
    p[((Xc - 0.28)**2 + (Yc + 0.30)**2) < 0.12**2] = 0.65
    p[(np.abs(Xc) < 0.06) & (np.abs(Yc) < 0.50)] = 0.2
    return p

moving = make_phantom(X, Y)   # canonical upright phantom (the moving image)

def resample(img, mapx, mapy):
    h, w = img.shape
    px = (mapx + 1) * (w - 1) / 2.0
    py = (mapy + 1) * (h - 1) / 2.0
    x0 = np.clip(np.floor(px).astype(int), 0, w - 2)
    y0 = np.clip(np.floor(py).astype(int), 0, h - 2)
    fx = np.clip(px - x0, 0, 1); fy = np.clip(py - y0, 0, 1)
    return ((1 - fx) * (1 - fy) * img[y0, x0] + fx * (1 - fy) * img[y0, x0 + 1]
            + (1 - fx) * fy * img[y0 + 1, x0] + fx * fy * img[y0 + 1, x0 + 1])

theta = np.deg2rad(20.0)
c, s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)
def rigid_part(x, y):
    return c * x - s * y + 0.14, s * x + c * y - 0.06
def affine_extra(x, y):
    return 1.18 * x + 0.17 * y, 0.84 * y
def deform_extra(x, y):
    return x + 0.12 * np.sin(2.4 * y) * np.exp(-(x**2)), y + 0.10 * np.sin(2.1 * x)
def true_map(x, y):
    xr, yr = rigid_part(x, y)
    xa, ya = affine_extra(xr, yr)
    return deform_extra(xa, ya)

# fixed = moving pulled through the full fixed->moving map (perfect recovery
# reproduces it exactly).
fixed = resample(moving, *true_map(X, Y))

est_stages = [
    ("identity (initial)", lambda x, y: (x, y), "#c0392b"),
    ("rigid_register", rigid_part, "#1f6f43"),
    ("+ affine_register", lambda x, y: affine_extra(*rigid_part(x, y)), "#1f5fa8"),
    ("+ greedy_syn (SyN)", true_map, "#6c3aa0"),
]

def grid_lines(ax, mapfun, color):
    g = np.linspace(-0.92, 0.92, 12)
    dense = np.linspace(-0.92, 0.92, 80)
    for v in g:
        mx, my = mapfun(np.full_like(dense, v), dense)
        ax.plot(mx, my, color=color, lw=0.55, alpha=0.8)
        mx, my = mapfun(dense, np.full_like(dense, v))
        ax.plot(mx, my, color=color, lw=0.55, alpha=0.8)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(13.5, 3.8))
for ax, (title, mapfun, col) in zip(axes, est_stages):
    warped = resample(moving, *mapfun(X, Y))
    ax.imshow(warped, cmap="gray", extent=[-1, 1, -1, 1], origin="lower",
              vmin=0, vmax=1)
    ax.contour(X, Y, fixed, levels=[0.25, 0.7], colors="#e8a020",
               linewidths=0.9, alpha=0.95)
    grid_lines(ax, mapfun, col)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10.5, color=col)
    ax.set_xlim(-1, 1); ax.set_ylim(-1, 1)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

fig.suptitle("syn_pipeline: warped moving (grey) closing onto the fixed edges "
             "(orange), the map growing rigid -> affine -> deformable",
             fontsize=11.5, y=1.03)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

`transform` says how far to climb: `'rigid'`, `'affine'`, or `'syn'` (default).
The per-stage `*_spec` / `force` arguments default to a **cross-modal-robust**
recipe — `MI(bins=32)` for the linear stages, the default `LNCCForce` SyN, the
`antsRegistrationSyN` convention. For a faster **within-modality** run, pass `SSD`
linear specs to engage the inverse-compositional fast path. `affine_restarts`
defaults to `4` for a histogram (MI / CR) affine metric and `1` otherwise. The
`PipelineResult` carries the composite `matrix`, the full `displacement` (or
`None` if the pipeline stopped at a linear stage), the `warped` image, and each
per-stage result (`rigid`, `affine`, `syn`) so you can inspect a stage's cost
history or Jacobian.

## SVF recipes: log-Demons and greedy SyN {#sec-svf-recipes}

When a linear map is not enough — two anatomies that differ by *bending*, not just
pose and scale — you need a **diffeomorphic** map: a smooth, invertible warp that
does not tear or fold. The two SVF recipes both produce one; the underlying math
(stationary velocity fields, the exponential by scaling-and-squaring, the BCH
update, fluid vs diffusion regularisation, the driving forces) is the subject of
[diffeomorphic](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf). Here we cover *which to reach for* and
*which knobs steer them*.

### When Demons, when SyN

`diffeomorphic_demons_register` is the **SSD** workhorse: a single velocity field,
driven by the closed-form **ESM (efficient symmetric) demons force**. Each
iteration is a per-voxel update — no inner solve — which is what makes it as
GPU-clean as it is. Reach for it when the two images are the **same modality**
with comparable intensities (one subject's two timepoints, a template and a
same-contrast subject).

`greedy_syn_register` is the **quality jump**: a *symmetric* formulation under the
**local cross-correlation (LNCC)** metric. It maintains *two* velocity fields,
$v_{\text{fwd}}$ (moving → midpoint) and $v_{\text{inv}}$ (fixed → midpoint), warps
both images to the shared midpoint and ascends the local CC there, then composes
the half-maps, $\varphi = (\mathrm{id}+s_{\text{fwd}}) \circ
(\mathrm{id}+s_{\text{inv}})^{-1}$. Two properties earn that extra machinery: LNCC
is robust to **smooth intensity inhomogeneity** (a bias field that defeats SSD),
and the symmetric construction removes the moving/fixed asymmetry (the result does
not depend on which image you called "moving"). Reach for it for **cross-subject
anatomy**, for any **bias-field** worry, and as the deformable stage of
`syn_pipeline`.

Both are pure `(moving, fixed) -> result` functions, so a **cohort** registers to
a shared reference with `jax.vmap`:

```python
jax.vmap(lambda m: greedy_syn_register(m, fixed, spec=spec))(moving_stack)
```

### The knobs

Both take `spec` (a `DemonsSpec` / `SyNSpec`), an optional `force`, the warm-start
pair `init_affine` / `init_displacement`, a `mask`, a `restrict` per-axis
deformation weight, the `winsorize` / `histogram_match` conditioning, and a
`smoothing_sigma`. The fields worth knowing:

- **`force`** — the driving term. `None` uses each recipe's default (ESM
  `DemonsForce(alpha)` for Demons, analytic `LNCCForce(radius)` for SyN). A single
  `Force` overrides it at every level — e.g. `MetricForce(MI())` for **cross-modal
  deformable** registration. A length-`levels` **coarse-to-fine** sequence sets a
  per-level schedule (a cheap force coarse, a high-signal one at the finest level).
- **`representation`** (`'group'` default | `'algebra'`). `'group'` carries the
  *displacement* and uses the greedy compositive update — warp directly, compose
  the regularised increment, no per-iteration `exp` (~2 gathers/iter for Demons vs
  ~7 for the algebra path; ~4 vs ~12 for SyN) — and recovers the velocity once at
  finalisation. `'algebra'` carries the *stationary velocity* and re-exponentiates
  every iteration: the exact-SVF path, the byte-identical parity oracle. Greedy is
  not the SVF fixed point, so the two agree on synthetic recovery *to tolerance*,
  not field-wise.
- **`n_steps`** — scaling-and-squaring steps for $\varphi = \exp(v)$ (6 Demons,
  5 SyN by default). See [diffeomorphic](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf).
- **`smoothing_sigma`** — a per-level Gaussian blur applied to each pyramid
  level *independently of the downsample* (ANTs `-s`, decoupled from the shrink
  `-f`): a scalar applies to every level, a length-`levels` **coarse-to-fine**
  sequence sets a schedule (e.g. `2x1x0` → `(2, 1, 0)`, heaviest at the coarsest
  level, none at the finest). `None` (default) leaves the pyramid's own
  anti-alias untouched. Smoothing the *intensities* widens the deformable basin
  the same way a coarser pyramid level does, without throwing away the grid.
- **`sigma_fluid` / `sigma_diffusion`** — the two Gaussian regularisers, the
  Green's functions of [the regularisers](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-regularisers).
  *Fluid* smooths each per-iteration update (a viscous-flow prior, allows large
  cumulative deformation); *diffusion* smooths the accumulated field (an
  elastic prior, keeps the total map smooth). Defaults `1.0` / `1.5`.
- **Demons-only:** `alpha` normalises the force (`denom = |J|^2 + α²(F−M∘φ)²`;
  larger $\alpha$ damps the step where the intensity difference is large);
  `bch_order` is the log-update order (`1` additive, `2` adds $\tfrac12[v,u]$, the
  [BCH](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-bch) correction).
- **SyN-only:** `radius` is the LNCC window ($2r+1$ per axis); `step` bounds the
  per-iteration voxel displacement; `step_mode` is `'clamp'` (default, a
  trust-region clamp $\min(1, \text{step}/\|u\|)$ plus a per-step Jacobian
  backtracking guard) or `'normalize'` (the ANTs scale-to convention, no Jacobian
  backtrack — the bounded smoothed step is diffeomorphic by construction).
- **`restrict`** — a length-`ndim` per-axis weight on the deformation (ANTs
  `--restrict-deformation`); a `0` suppresses deformation along that axis, e.g.
  `(1, 1, 0)` for in-plane-only.
- **`init_affine` / `init_displacement`** (at most one) — pre-warp `moving` onto
  the `fixed` grid and register the **residual**; the returned `displacement` /
  `warped` / `jacobian_det` are the **total** (init then residual) map. This is how
  `syn_pipeline` feeds the affine matrix into SyN.

Both return a `jacobian_det` map — $\det J$ of the deformation. **All-positive
means no folding**: this is your quality assurance that the recovered map is a
genuine diffeomorphism (the validation posture of the whole suite). The `velocity`
output (Demons) / `forward_velocity` + `inverse_velocity` (SyN) is `None` by
default — `compute_velocity=False` skips the `field_log` recovery, which under
`jit` is never even traced (no compile, no runtime). Set `compute_velocity=True`
only when you need the velocity itself.

::: {.callout-warning title="Backend reproducibility"}
The Gaussian regulariser dispatches engines per backend (parallel FIR on GPU,
recursive Young-van Vliet on CPU), which differ by ~1-2 % near the edges. A pair
can therefore yield slightly different deformations on GPU vs CPU (recovery
accuracy is unaffected). For a reproducible study, register every subject of a
cohort on the **same backend**; do not mix CPU- and GPU-computed warps in one
analysis.
:::

## Motion realignment: `volreg` {#sec-volreg}

`volreg(series, *, reference, passes, spec, space, method)` is the `3dvolreg` /
`mcflirt` task: **batched rigid realignment** of a `(T, *spatial)` series to a
common reference. It is `rigid_register` lifted over a frame axis, with one
important optimisation — the work that depends only on the reference is done
**once** and the per-frame solve is `vmap`-ed over the series.

The motivating question is the moving fMRI series: across a scan the subject's head
drifts, so each frame sits at a slightly different rigid pose. Realignment recovers
a per-frame rigid transform and resamples every frame onto one grid.

**The reference.** `reference='mean'` (default) is the per-voxel mean over frames;
an `int` picks a frame index; an array is an explicit reference. The mean of an
*unaligned* series is blurred, which is exactly why the **two-pass schedule**
exists: `passes=2` runs once, re-references to the mean of the *first-pass
realignment* (now sharp), warm-starts each frame from its first-pass parameters,
and runs again — the standard robustness step. The final `reference` is returned in
the result.

**The hoisting.** Per pass, the reference's Gaussian pyramid is built once; on the
inverse-compositional path its steepest-descent images and Hessian are built once
too (`ic_reference`). Then a single `per_frame` function is `jax.vmap`-ed over the
frame axis with that shared reference work captured — so the whole series compiles
*once*, and the expensive reference linearisation is amortised across all $T$
frames. This is the structural reason `volreg` is fast.

**SSD-only.** The batched path uses the matrix-free Gauss-Newton / Levenberg-
Marquardt solver, which `vmap`s cleanly over frames; the scalar BFGS metrics do
not. So `volreg` **requires a least-squares metric** (SSD, the default) and raises
otherwise. `method` is `"auto"` (the inverse-compositional constant-template
Hessian in `IndexSpace`, with the reference work hoisted; the forward GN path
otherwise), `"inverse_compositional"` (force IC, `IndexSpace`-only), or
`"forward"`. `space` carries the per-image voxel → world geometry, so motion that
is rigid in *physical* space is recovered correctly on anisotropic grids
(`WorldSpace`).

::: {.callout-note title="Tune the schedule — motion is a small-displacement problem"}
Motion realignment moves each frame by a *small* amount, and a second-order solver
converges in a handful of steps. The `RegistrationSpec` default of 30 iterations
per level **over-iterates motion correction by ~25×**. A tight schedule —
`iterations=(4, 2, 1)` over a 3-level pyramid — realigns *identically* for a
fraction of the cost (a 20-frame MNI-2mm series realigns the same at `(4,2,1)` for
~1/8 the time). The tuple is **coarse-to-fine**: front-load the cheap coarse
levels, starve the expensive finest one.
:::

`spec.mode='early_exit'` opts into a **batch-aggregate** early-exit on the
inverse-compositional path: the per-frame `lax.while_loop` runs under `vmap` until
*all* frames have converged, so the cohort adapts to the actual motion (a looser
`convergence.threshold` ~1e-3 gives ~2-3× at identical realignment). It is not
reverse-differentiable and is rejected on the forward path (which threads only the
fixed scan). `VolregResult` carries the per-frame `matrices` `(T, d+1, d+1)`,
`params` `(T, p)`, the `realigned` series `(T, *spatial)`, the `reference`, and the
per-frame `cost_history`.

## Boundary-based registration: `bbr_register` {#sec-bbr}

The cross-modal within-subject case has a special structure worth exploiting.
An **EPI** (the fMRI workhorse) and the subject's **T1** are the *same* head, so
the transform between them is rigid — but their intensities are unrelated (CSF is
bright in one, dark in the other), so a same-intensity metric is useless. The
Greve-Fischl insight is to stop matching intensities and instead **align a tissue
boundary to where the image contrast is sharpest**. You already know the WM/GM
surface from the T1; a correct rigid pose places that surface exactly on the strong
intensity edge in the EPI.

`bbr_register(moving, points, normals, *, moving_affine, init_params, spec)` does
this. Note there is **no `fixed` image** — the target is a *boundary*, given as
arrays of `points` and outward unit `normals` (surfaces as a data structure are out
of scope; BBR consumes the samples). Because the cost is over boundary points, not
an image pair, BBR is a sibling `Objective` (`BoundaryObjective`), not a `Metric`.

**The cost.** For each boundary point, sample the moving intensity a short distance
$\pm\text{step}$ along the (transformed) surface normal — one sample each side of
the boundary — and form the **normalised cross-boundary contrast**

$$
Q = \frac{I_{\text{out}} - I_{\text{in}}}
         {\tfrac12\,(I_{\text{in}} + I_{\text{out}}) + \varepsilon}.
$$ {#eq-recipes-bbr-q}

$I_{\text{in}}$ / $I_{\text{out}}$ are the inside / outside samples and
$\varepsilon$ guards the denominator. Because $Q$ is a *ratio*, it is invariant to a
global intensity scale — the recovery is image-amplitude independent. A correct
alignment puts a strong, consistently-oriented edge across every boundary point, so
the cost rewards large $|Q|$ of the expected sign:

$$
\mathcal{C} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n} \bigl(1 + \tanh\bigl(\text{slope}\cdot(Q_n - q_0)\bigr)\bigr).
$$ {#eq-recipes-bbr-cost}

The $\tanh$ saturates — once a point sits on a good edge, pushing further does not
keep paying — and `slope` / `q0` (in `BBRSpec`) set its sharpness and inflection.
`bbr_cost(...)` is the pure, differentiable core; both `BoundaryObjective.cost` and
the implicit-diff layer call it. The rotation is taken about the **boundary
centroid**, not the origin, so the rotation parameter's leverage is the structure
radius — not the distance to the voxel origin, which would dominate the
conditioning.

**Recovery (the basin is narrow and non-convex).** Two ingredients, mirroring
FSL's `flirt --bbr`, make recovery robust without a fixed-image pyramid:

- **Step annealing** (`BBRSpec.schedule`, e.g. `(4.0, 2.0, 1.0)`). A *large* step
  samples far either side of the boundary, which smooths the cost and **widens its
  basin** — it captures the gross translation a fine step is blind to. Finer stages
  then refine, each warm-started from the previous. This is the diffeomorphic
  pyramid's coarse-to-fine idea, realised through the sampling distance rather than
  image resolution. `None` runs a single stage at `spec.step`.
- **Grid multistart** (`BBRSpec.search`, a `BBRSearch`). Before refining, the cost
  is evaluated on a coarse Cartesian grid of rigid seeds around `init_params`
  (over $\pm$`rotation` rad per rotation DOF and $\pm$`translation` per translation
  DOF), and the lowest-cost seed initialises the solve — so a single local descent
  cannot confidently latch a *wrong* boundary alignment. The grid is
  $\text{steps}^{n_{\text{params}}}$ seeds under one `vmap` ($3^6 = 729$ for a 3-D
  rigid at the default) — cheap on a GPU. The argmin is `stop_gradient`-ed (never
  back-propagate the discrete seed choice), and NaN seed costs are guarded so an
  off-grid seed cannot win.

**The optimiser** is a *normalised-block gradient descent*, not Newton. The
rotation and translation gradient blocks are unit-normalised separately and stepped
by physical step lengths — `rotation_step` (rad) and `translation_step` (mm) — with
a geometric `step_decay`. This decouples the step from the image amplitude *and*
from the rad-vs-mm unit mismatch that defeats a naive single-rate Newton step on
this non-convex cost. It runs through the shared `run_iterations` driver, so
`BBRSpec.mode` gives the same `'fixed'` (scan, the BBR default) / `'early_exit'`
(while-loop) toggle as every other recipe. For a reverse-mode gradient of the
optimum, take it through the implicit-function layer
(`linalg.implicit_minimize` on `bbr_cost`), whose adjoint is solved at the optimum
and is trajectory-independent — preferred over differentiating the unrolled scan.

`moving_affine` (voxel → world) makes `step` a physical (mm) distance and the
normals physical directions — correct under anisotropy; `None` defaults to the
voxel frame. Orient the normals so the interior ($-\text{normal}$) side is the
brighter tissue. BBR is a **local refinement of a prior affine**: the default
multistart + annealing widen the capture range to only a few mm / degrees, so pass
`init_params` from a prior affine for a larger initial misalignment.
`BBRResult` carries the recovered rigid `matrix` (surface frame → moving frame,
world → world with an affine, else index-space), `params`, the final `cost`, and
the concatenated `cost_history`. The absolute cost scale *shifts* between annealing
stages — a finer step yields a higher cost — so the trace is monotone *within* a
stage, not necessarily across the concatenation, just as the per-level pyramid
traces are.

## The spec gallery and the decision table {#sec-specs}

Every recipe is configured by a **frozen dataclass** and returns a **`NamedTuple`**.
The pattern is deliberate (the design's static-config / pure-output tenet): a frozen
spec is hashable, so it rides a `jit` static argument without retracing; a
`NamedTuple` output is a plain PyTree of arrays, so it `vmap`s, differentiates, and
serialises with no custom machinery. You build a spec once and pass it in; you never
mutate it.

### The specs at a glance

`RegistrationSpec` (rigid / affine / volreg) — defaults:

| field | default | what it does |
|---|---|---|
| `levels` | `3` | Gaussian-pyramid resolutions (coarse-to-fine) |
| `iterations` | `30` | per-level count; `int` or a coarse-to-fine tuple |
| `metric` | `SSD()` | similarity: `SSD` / `LNCC(radius)` / `MI(bins)` / `CorrelationRatio(bins)` |
| `optimizer` | `'auto'` | LSQ → `lm`; else `bfgs`; or force `lm`/`gn`/`bfgs` |
| `interpolation` | `Linear()` | warp kernel |
| `boundary_mode`, `cval` | `'constant'`, `0.0` | out-of-bounds handling |
| `pyramid_factor`, `pyramid_sigma` | `2.0`, `None` | downsample factor / anti-alias |
| `cg_tol` | `1e-6` | inner-CG tolerance (GN/LM) |
| `mode`, `convergence` | `'fixed'`, `Convergence()` | iteration strategy + early-exit threshold/window |
| `ic_line_search` | `False` | opt-in cost-decrease guard on the IC step |

`SVFSpec` (the base both deformable specs embed) — defaults: `levels=3`,
`iterations=80`, `sigma_fluid=1.0`, `sigma_diffusion=1.5`, `spacing=None`,
`pyramid_factor=2.0`, `boundary_mode='nearest'`, `representation='group'`,
`mode='fixed'`, `compute_velocity=False`. `DemonsSpec` adds `n_steps=6`,
`alpha=0.4`, `bch_order=1`. `SyNSpec` adds `radius=2`, `step=0.25`,
`step_mode='clamp'`, `n_steps=5`.

`BBRSpec` — defaults: `step=1.0`, `slope=0.5`, `q0=0.0`, `iterations=50`,
`schedule=(4.0, 2.0, 1.0)`, `search=BBRSearch()`, `mode='fixed'`,
`rotation_step=0.03`, `translation_step=2.0`, `step_decay=0.92`,
`interpolation=Linear()`, `boundary_mode='nearest'`, `eps=1e-3`. The nested
`BBRSearch` defaults to `rotation=0.07`, `translation=4.0`, `steps=3`, `step=None`.

`Convergence` (parameterises `mode='early_exit'`, inert under `'fixed'`):
`threshold=1e-6`, `window=10`.

### The outputs

| recipe | output | key fields |
|---|---|---|
| `rigid_register`, `affine_register` | `RegistrationResult` | `matrix`, `params`, `warped`, `cost_history` |
| `syn_pipeline` | `PipelineResult` | `matrix`, `displacement`, `warped`, `rigid`/`affine`/`syn` |
| `volreg` | `VolregResult` | `matrices`, `params`, `realigned`, `reference`, `cost_history` |
| `bbr_register` | `BBRResult` | `matrix`, `params`, `cost`, `cost_history` |
| `diffeomorphic_demons_register` | `DiffeomorphicResult` | `velocity`, `displacement`, `warped`, `jacobian_det`, `cost_history` |
| `greedy_syn_register` | `SyNResult` | `forward_velocity`, `inverse_velocity`, `displacement`, `warped`, `jacobian_det`, `cost_history` |

### The decision table

The same routing as @fig-recipes-decision, with the deciding axes spelled out —
modality (same / cross), transform (linear / deformable), domain
(volume / surface), and what you are optimising for:

| situation | recipe | spec / knob |
|---|---|---|
| same-modality, linear, fast | `rigid_register` / `affine_register` | `SSD` + `IndexSpace` → IC fast path; `method='auto'` |
| large misalignment, linear | `affine_register` | `init='moment'` or warm-start from rigid; `restarts>1` for multi-basin |
| anisotropic voxels / differing grids | any matrix recipe | `space=WorldSpace(fixed_affine, moving_affine)` |
| cross-modal, linear | `rigid_register` / `affine_register` | `MI(bins)` or `CorrelationRatio(bins)` (forward path) |
| EPI ↔ T1 within-subject | `bbr_register` | boundary points + normals; warm-start from a prior affine |
| 4-D series motion | `volreg` | `SSD`; `iterations=(4,2,1)`; `passes=2` for a blurred mean ref |
| same-modality deformable | `diffeomorphic_demons_register` | `DemonsSpec`; `force=DemonsForce(alpha)` |
| cross-subject / bias-field deformable | `greedy_syn_register` | `SyNSpec`; default `LNCCForce(radius)` |
| cross-modal deformable | Demons / SyN | `force=MetricForce(MI())` |
| full T1 → MNI | `syn_pipeline` | `transform='syn'`; `MI` linear + LNCC SyN (default) |
| moving a label map / atlas through a result | `apply_transform` | integer image → `NearestNeighbour` auto; `MultiLabel` for anti-aliased |
| differentiable registration layer | any (`mode='fixed'`) | keep `mode='fixed'`; or use the `linalg.implicit_*` entry points |

## Further reading

- Cox, R. W. & Jesmanowicz, A. (1999). *Real-time 3D image registration for
  functional MRI*. Magnetic Resonance in Medicine 42(6) — the `3dvolreg` rigid /
  motion-correction lineage.
- Jenkinson, M., Bannister, P., Brady, M. & Smith, S. (2002). *Improved
  optimization for the robust and accurate linear registration and motion
  correction of brain images*. NeuroImage 17(2) — FLIRT / MCFLIRT.
- Baker, S. & Matthews, I. (2004). *Lucas-Kanade 20 years on: a unifying
  framework*. International Journal of Computer Vision 56(3) — the
  inverse-compositional scheme behind the fast matrix path.
- Greve, D. N. & Fischl, B. (2009). *Accurate and robust brain image alignment
  using boundary-based registration*. NeuroImage 48(1) — BBR.
- Vercauteren, T., Pennec, X., Perchant, A. & Ayache, N. (2009). *Diffeomorphic
  demons: efficient non-parametric image registration*. NeuroImage 45(1 Suppl) —
  the log-domain ESM demons recipe.
- Avants, B. B., Epstein, C. L., Grossman, M. & Gee, J. C. (2008). *Symmetric
  diffeomorphic image registration with cross-correlation*. Medical Image Analysis
  12(1) — greedy SyN.
- Avants, B. B., Tustison, N. J., Song, G., et al. (2011). *A reproducible
  evaluation of ANTs similarity metric performance in brain image registration*.
  NeuroImage 54(3) — the `antsRegistrationSyN` staged convention.

## See also

- `rigid_register` / `affine_register` / `apply_transform` / `syn_pipeline`:
  [`register/recipes.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/recipes.py)
- `volreg`:
  [`register/_volreg.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_volreg.py)
- `bbr_register` / `bbr_cost` / `BoundaryObjective`:
  [`register/_bbr.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_bbr.py)
- `diffeomorphic_demons_register` / `DemonsSpec`:
  [`register/diffeomorphic.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/diffeomorphic.py)
- `greedy_syn_register` / `SyNSpec`:
  [`register/_syn.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_syn.py)
- `RegistrationSpec` / `RegistrationResult` / the driver:
  [`register/_core.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_core.py)
- the public surface:
  [`register/__init__.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/__init__.py)
- Neighbouring pages: [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-ladder),
  [transforms](transforms.qmd#sec-lie-exp),
  [optimization](optimization.qmd#sec-inverse-comp),
  [diffeomorphic](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf),
  [similarity](similarity.qmd#sec-mi), [performance](performance.qmd#sec-batching).
